coredata.dc:description: Constructing heterojunctions with matched band semiconductor is regarded as effective strategy to promote high-efficiency photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Herein, 0D/2D direct Z-scheme heterojunction involving carbonized polymer dots and Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets (CPDs/Bi4O5Br2) is designed and fabricated, which effectively facilitate migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and retain more negative electron reduction potential of CPDs and more positive hole oxidation potential of Bi4O5Br2. Moreover, CPDs promote adsorption of CO2 and intermediate COOH* as well as desorption of product CO. The direct Z-scheme mechanism of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 is collaboratively confirmed by theory calculation, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and time-resolved transient absorption spectroscopy. The 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 exhibits the maximal CO production of 132.42 μmol h−1g−1 under Xe lamp irradiation, 5.43 fold higher than that of Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets. The CPDs with up-conversion properties can broaden light utilization range, so that composite material also show better CO2 conversion performance when excitation wavelength is greater than 580 nm.

(d) TEM and (e-g) HRTEM images of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2. (h-i) the corresponding fast Fourier transformation (FFT) images of f and g. The scale of f and g is 1 nm. The scale of h and i is 5 1/nm.

High-resolution (a) O 1s and (b) Br 3d XPS spectra of Bi4O5Br2 and 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2. High-resolution (c) N 1s XPS spectra of CPDs and 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2. The calculated work function and corresponding structural model of the (d) (020) plane of Bi4O5Br2, (e) (100) plane of CPDs and (f) CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction.

(a) Transient absorption spectrum at 20 ps time delay of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 under irradiation with a 400 nm laser flash, time profiles of TAS at (b) 470 nm and (c) 620 nm. (d) Schematic illustration of the photoexcited electron transfer pathway for CPDs/Bi4O5Br2.

(a) EIS spectra and transient photocurrent spectra under (b) Xe lamp and (c) λ ≥ 580 nm light irradiation for these materials. (d) CO2 adsorption isotherms of Bi4O5Br2 and 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constructing heterojunctions with matched band semiconductor is regarded as effective strategy to promote high-efficiency photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Herein, 0D/2D direct Z-scheme heterojunction involving carbonized polymer dots and Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets (CPDs/Bi4O5Br2) is designed and fabricated, which effectively facilitate migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and retain more negative electron reduction potential of CPDs and more positive hole oxidation potential of Bi4O5Br2. Moreover, CPDs promote adsorption of CO2 and intermediate COOH* as well as desorption of product CO. The direct Z-scheme mechanism of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 is collaboratively confirmed by theory calculation, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and time-resolved transient absorption spectroscopy. The 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 exhibits the maximal CO production of 132.42 μmol h−1g−1 under Xe lamp irradiation, 5.43 fold higher than that of Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets. The CPDs with up-conversion properties can broaden light utilization range, so that composite material also show better CO2 conversion performance when excitation wavelength is greater than 580 nm.

ce:section.ce:para: Simulating the photosynthesis of plants and using sunlight as the driving force to convert CO2 into renewable carbon-based fuels is an effective way to alleviate energy crises and environmental pollution [1]. Regrettably, the conversion efficiency of photocatalytic CO2 reduction is still unsatisfactory. This is attributed to the low efficiency of photocatalytic photocarrier separation and sluggish CO2 adsorption and activation capacity [2,3]. Moreover, traditional TiO2 and ZnO can absorb ultraviolet light, which accounts for only ∼4% of the sunlight [4,5]. Most of the reported visible light-driven semiconductor catalysts (such as: CdS [6], carbon nitride [7], WO3 [8], silver halide [9], etc.) are excited by light with wavelength less than 500 nm. Therefore, the utilization of visible light (∼46 % of sunlight, 400 nm < λ < 760 nm) is still very low. For this reason, constructing photocatalysts with wide spectral absorption, high carrier separation efficiency and enriching active sites are conducive to the rapid development of photocatalytic CO2 conversion.

Recently, bismuth-based oxyhalides as recently developed semiconductor photocatalysts possess adjustable solar absorption range, allowing the feasible reduction ability of photoexcited electrons for water splitting, nitrogen fixation and carbon dioxide reduction [10,11]. However, single bismuth oxyhalide photocatalysts have high recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers [12,13]. It is difficult to provide enough photogenerated electrons for CO2 conversion, resulting in low photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of monomer materials. Although a series of strategies (such as defect engineering [14], heteroatom doping [15], morphology control [16], semiconductor heterojunction [17], etc) have been applied to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge, it is still challenging to obtain an efficient photocatalytic system with the merits of high visible light utilization, effective charge separation, and strong redox ability simultaneously [18,19]. Some studies have shown that the direct Z-scheme heterojunction through the internal electric field could not only greatly facilitate the separation of photo-generated carriers, but also preserve the original strong redox capability of the carriers, which could greatly enhance the conversion of photocarriers into active species for high-efficiency photocatalytic reactions [20,21]. Therefore, selecting suitable band semiconductors to construct heterojunction system could be regarded as one of the effective approaches to cope with the challenge on the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), as an emerging carbon-based low-dimensional nanomaterials have been widely used in photocatalysis due to their adjustable band structure, excellent photoelectric properties, high photo-chemical stability and facile preparation [22,23]. The abundance of surface polar groups (carboxyl, amino or hydroxyl) allows CPDs to easily combine with semiconductors to construct heterojunction structures, which can observe enhanced carrier separation efficiency and visible light utilization in hybrid photocatalysts and witnessing outstanding charge-transfer ability of CPDs [24–26]. Compared to the traditional up-conversion materials such as Yb and Tm, the light responsiveness of CPDs is not limited to a specific wavelength. The CPDs exhibit wide range of up-conversion absorption and large cross-section of the PL emission spectra, which strikingly facilitate the energy bandgap matching of a vast majority of photocatalysts. Combining the tunable energy band structure of bismuth-based oxyhalides and CPDs, the Z-scheme composite semiconductor photocatalytic system is designed, which is expected to obtain high-efficiency visible light-driven CO2 conversion materials.

Inspired by the above-mentioned advantages, the direct Z-scheme composite photocatalysts were constructed by combining the bismuth-based oxyhalides Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs with energy band matching. The direct Z-scheme mechanism of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 is confirmed by theory calculation, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and the time-resolved transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) in detail. The up-conversion properties of CPDs effectively broadened the use of visible light and the construction of direct Z-scheme mechanism material further contributed to the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs under λ ≥ 580 nm light irradiation. Moreover, the introduction of CPDs enhanced the effective adsorption of CO2 and intermediates (COOH*) for the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite. The formation of Z-scheme heterojunction in the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite catalyst, which constructs a new carrier transmission path and preserves the original strong redox capability of the carriers and then significantly improved the separation of photogenerated carriers to boost efficient photocatalytic CO2 conversion. The 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 exhibited the maximal production of CO, attaining the generation rate of 132.42 μmol h−1 g−1 under Xe lamp, the activity of which is much higher than that of Bi4O5Br2 monomer and other composite system (Table S1-S2). This work provide new ideas for the preparation of highly efficient visible light-driven CO2 conversion materials.

All the regents are directly used as purchased without further purification. Citric acid monohydrate (AR, ≥ 99.5 %), ethylenediamine (AR, ≥ 99.0 %), Bi(NO3)3 (AR, ≥ 99.0 %), hexamethylenetetramine (AR ≥ 99.0 %), mannitol (AR), were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

The CPDs was prepared according to the reported literature [22]. Typically, 5 mmol citric acid and 335 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved into 10 mL deionized water. The mixed solution was added into 25 mL Teflonlined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the acquired product was subjected to dialysis for 24 h in order to obtain the CPDs solution. The CPDs solution was further freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain CPDs solid.

In a typical procedure of Bi4O5Br2 catalysts, 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 5 mmol hexamethylenetetramine were dissolved into 40 mL mannitol aqueous solution (0.1 M), which defined as solution A. 1 mmol KBr was dissolved into 10 mL mannitol aqueous solution (0.1 M) to obtain solution B. Then solution B was dropwise added into solution A with stirring continuously. After stirring for 30 min, the mixed solution continued to stir in a 90 °C oil bath for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected and washed with ethanol and deionized water for several times. The final Bi4O5Br2 product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

In a typical procedure of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 catalysts, 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 5 mmol hexamethylenetetramine and x g CPDs were dissolved into 40 mL mannitol aqueous solution (0.1 M), which defined as solution A. 1 mmol KBr was dissolved into 10 mL mannitol aqueous solution (0.1 M) to obtain solution B. Then solution B was dropwise added into solution A with stirring continuously. After stirring for 30 min, the mixed solution continued to stir in a 90 °C oil bath for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected and washed with ethanol and deionized water for several times. The final CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 products were dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The mass percentage of CPDs in CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 materials were 5, 8 and 10 wt %, respectively.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern measured on Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer with monochromatized Cu-Kα radiation. The Fourier transform spectrophotometer (FT-IR, Nexus 470, Thermo Electron Corporation) analyzed via the standard KBr disk method. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measured on a PHI5300 with monochromatic Mg Kα source. The specific surface area and particle size of samplesmobtained using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method based on the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Micromeritics Instrument Corporarion, USA). The UV–vis spectra detected by UV–vis spectrometer on an UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The morphology of these materials investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JEOL JSM-7001 F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-JEM-2010) equip. CO2 adsorption isotherms measurements of these samples carried out CO2 adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics Instrument Corporarion, USA). The photocurrent, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky plots measured in phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M PBS, pH = 7.0), 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4- and 0.1 M KCl solution, respectively, and recorded with the CHI 760E electrochemical system.

The ultrafast spectroscopy employed a femtosecond amplifier laser system (Hurricane, Spectra Physics) that generating laser pulses with 1 kHz repetition rate, 800 nm central wavelength and ∼100 fs pulse duration. The output was split into two beams. The first beam was frequency doubled to generate 400 nm excitation pulses. The second beam with weaker energy was focused into a sapphire plate to generate white light continuum for visible probe. The time delay between the pump beam and the probe beam was controlled by a motorized delay stage, and both of two beams were focused onto the sample. After passing through the sample, the excitation-induced transmission change of the probe light was collected by an optical fiber spectrometer (AvaSpec-ULS2048CL-EVO, Avantes).

The in-situ FT-IR spectra obtained using in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (Thermo fisher Nicolet iZ10, USA). Before testing, the catalyst surface was cleaned with He atmosphere at 120 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the background was measured in darkness. Then, CO2 and water vapor were introduced in the darkness. The adsorption/desorption equilibrium of catalyst and reaction source maintained for a period of time. Subsequently, the FT-IR spectrum will be collected under different light irradiation time.

The photocatalytic CO2 reduction test carried out in the full glass automatic on-line trace gas analysis system (Labsolar-6A, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.). Typically, 30 mg of the photocatalysts and 10 mL of H2O were added into the reactor. After the reactor is evacuated, the high purity CO2 (purity: 99.999 %) was introduced. The pressure of CO2 in the system was adjusted to 80 kPa, and the reaction temperature was kept at 10 °C using low-temperature thermostat bath system. The light source respectively was 300 W Xenon lamp source (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.), and 300 W Xenon lamp source with 400 nm or 580 nm cutoff filter. After each reaction, the products were analyzed using gas chromatography (Zhejiang FuLi Chromatograph Instruments Co., Itd. GC-2002) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID), thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and capillary column. C1 gas products (CO and CH4), H2 and O2 separated via Hayesep A and 5A molecular sieves chromatographic column, then tested by FID and TCD. Multi-carbon gas products (such as ethylene, ethane, etc.) separated by Hayesep A and Porapak N chromatographic columns and detected by the FID. The test results show that the product has only CO and the selectivity reaches 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology characterization of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The reddish brown CPDs have been prepared by aggregation of citric acid and ethylenediamine [27]. As shown in Fig. S1a, the CPDs exhibited broad diffraction peaks at around 24°, indexed to (002) crystal planes of graphitic structure, in line with typical characteristic peak of amorphous carbon material [28]. They showed strong photoluminescence under ultraviolet irradiation (the inset of Fig. S1a). Fig. S1b and S1c are the down-converted and up-converted PL spectra of the CPDs, respectively. As is shown, when the excitation wavelength is greater than 500 nm, the CPDs can emit light less than 500 nm, which can in turn excite visible light photocatalysts to generate electron-hole pairs. TEM images display the diameter of CPDs is about 5 nm (Fig. S1d). Lattice fringe spacing with 0.211 nm is correspond to the (100) crystal plane of CPDs (the inset of Fig. S1d). The elemental composition and bonding configurations of the CPDs were quantified by XPS measurements (Fig. S2). The CPDs are mainly composed of three elements: C, N and O (Fig. S2a). The deconvolution of C 1s band is three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.6 eV and 287.8 eV corresponding to CC/C=C, CO/C-N and CO, respectively (Fig. S2b). The N 1s analysis reveals the presence of CN (399.6 eV) and NH (401.3 eV) (Fig. S2c). The O 1s band contains two peaks at 531.2 and 532.6 eV for CO and CO, respectively (Fig. S2d). The above experimental characterization indicated that CPDs have been successfully prepared.

The phase structures of the obtained samples were examined by XRD analysis (Fig. 1 a). All these XRD diffraction peaks are indexed to the monoclinic Bi4O5Br2 (JCPDS card no. 37-0699). There is no obvious characteristic peak of CPDs, possibly because of low content of CPDs in the composites [29,30]. To further determine the existence of CPDs, these materials are observed by FT-IR spectra. As is shown in Fig. 1b, the peak at 540 cm−1 is associated with the Bi-O stretching mode [30], which appears in pure Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs/Bi4O5Br2. The peaks at 1770, 1653 and 1521 cm−1 are associated with the -C = O-, -C = N and -N-H of CPDs, respectively [23], which confirms the presence of CPDs in CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites. Furthermore, the Raman and XPS results also indicate the existence of CPDs in CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites (Figs. S3 and S4, the detailed data analysis in supporting information).

The optical properties of as-prepared samples are measured by the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 1c). The pristine Bi4O5Br2 showed the abrupt onset of absorption at nearly 500 nm, owing to electronic transition from valence band to conduction band of Bi4O5Br2 under light irradiation. As the content of CPDs increases, the absorption intensity in the range of 200−800 nm for the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite materials gradually increases. The (αEphoton )1/2 vs Ephoton curves of pure Bi4O5Br2 calculated by the classical Tauc approach, indicates the band gap energy of Bi4O5Br2 is nearly 2.26 eV (Fig. S5). The positive slopes of Mott-Schotty plots reveal Bi4O5Br2 is an n-type semiconductor (Fig. S6), and the flat band potential of Bi4O5Br2 is about -0.62 V vs. NHE (pH = 7) according to the extrapolation of X intercept in these Mott-Schotty curves [31]. The Fermi level for the n-type semiconductor approximates the flat band potential. As the valence band XPS spectra with the Fermi level of semiconductors is 0 eV (Fig. S7), the valence band of Bi4O5Br2 is 1.30 V vs. NHE. Based on the formula ECB = EVB - Eg , the conduction band of Bi4O5Br2 is about -0.96 V vs. NHE. Previous research reported the valence band and conduction band of CPDs was -0.56 and -1.23 V vs. NHE, respectively [23]. Therefore, both Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs have the suitable reduction potential to drive the photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (Fig. S8) [32].

The morphology of pure Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 are examined by TEM (Fig. S9−10, 1d). As is show in Fig. S9, the Bi4O5Br2 exhibits ultra-thin sheet-like structure with the average thickness about 2 nm. The HRTEM image shows that the lattice spacing is determined to be 0.282 nm (Fig. S9b insert), which corresponds to the (020) crystal plane of Bi4O5Br2. It's worth noting that the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite still maintains an ultra-thin structure (Fig. S10). As expected, the CPDs are evenly distributed on the surface of Bi4O5Br2 material (Fig. 1d). The lattice fringe spacing of 0.211 nm and 0.282 nm are in accordance with the (100) crystal plane of CPDs and the (020) crystallographic planes of Bi4O5Br2 crystallites [33], respectively, indicating the heterojunction structure is constructed successfully (Fig. 1e–i). The elemental mapping of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite showed that the elements of Bi, O, Br, C and N were all found in the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites. It can prove all the elements are well dispersed and confirm the CPDs and Bi4O5Br2 were well combined (Fig. S11). This result further proves the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites were synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on Z-scheme mechanism of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 material
ce:section.ce:para: In photocatalytic composite system, as the formation of heterogeneous interface causes distortion of the Fermi level, the chemical state of elements and flow direction of electrons in the composite material will change, which could been investigated by XPS spectroscopy [34,35]. As shown in Figs. 2 , S4 and S12, compared with pure Bi4O5Br2, the Bi 4f peak of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 does not change (Fig. S4b), while the O 1s and Br 3d peaks of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 have shifted negatively (Fig. 2a–b). In comparison with pristine CPDs, the C 1s peak of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 does not change (Fig. S12), while the N 1s peak of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 has a positive shift (Fig. 2c). The previous study found that the valence band of Bi4O5Br2 is mainly composed of O 2p and Br 4p orbitals [36]. And the N atoms doped in CPDs contain unpaired lone pairs of electrons [22,37]. When CPDs and Bi4O5Br2 material are combined to form heterogeneous interface, the electrons will transfer from N atoms in CPDs component to the O and Br atoms in Bi4O5Br2 component, thus forming the internal electric field at interfaces pointing from CPDs to Bi4O5Br2, and facilitating the construction of direct Z-scheme CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction without any redox mediator (Fig. S13) [34,35,38].

In addition, the work function (Φ) is another important parameter for revealing electron transfer in compound heterostructures semiconductor material, which can be calculated from the energy difference between the vacuum level and Fermi level, according to the electrostatic potential of these materials. As is shown in Fig. 2d–f, the work functions of Bi4O5Br2 (020), CPDs (100) and CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction are 7.58, 5.01 and 7.04 eV, respectively, indicating lower Fermi levels of Bi4O5Br2 (020) surface than that of CPDs (100) surface. The Fermi level of the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction is between the two monomers. When CPDs are closely contacted with Bi4O5Br2 without illumination, the electrons would flow from CPDs to Bi4O5Br2 to enable the balanced same Fermi level and the internal electric field at the interface between Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs would be created (Fig. S13). The theoretical calculation results of work function are absolutely consistent with above XPS results.

In order to deeply clarify the effective interface charge transfer through the direct Z-scheme approach in the heterogeneous junction, the TAS was performed to track the real-time photogenerated carrier dynamics of this as-prepared materials [39,40]. The photogenerated electron kinetics are revealed by the 400 nm pump excitation with 0.5 u J. As is shown in Fig. S14a and c, the transient absorption signal peaks of Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs materials are negative and positive, respectively. The transient absorption spectrum of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 has both positive and negative signal peaks (Fig. 3 a), which indicates the photogenerated charge of Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs in the composite material can be tracked separately. The decay kinetics of photo-generated carrier for these materials have been further estimated. The time profiles of the TAS probed at specific wavelength (Transient absorption signal at 470 nm corresponds to Bi4O5Br2, signal at 620 nm corresponds to CPDs) were fitted by the exponential function (Figs. 3b–c, S14b and d). In the wavelength of 470 nm, the transient absorption signal belongs to the decay path of the photo-generated carriers for Bi4O5Br2 material. The short lifetime (τ1) and long lifetime (τ2) are assigned to the photoexcited electrons that undergo a fast relaxation from CB to exciton-mediated trap state and the excited state electrons recombine with the holes on the nanosecond time scale, respectively [41,42]. Compared with the original Bi4O5Br2 (τ1 = 100 ± 60 ps (36 %), τ2 > 5 ns (64 %)) (Fig. S14b), the τ1 decay lifetime of Bi4O5Br2 in 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite material is largely decreased to 2 ± 1 ps (56 %) (Fig. 3b). The photoexcited electrons relaxation ratio of the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite material from CB to exciton-mediated trap state is significantly reduced, which indicates the existence of an additional channel of interfacial for electrons from CB of excited Bi4O5Br2 to the VB of CPDs (Fig. 3d) [43]. Meanwhile, compared with the pure CPDs (τ1 = 1.9 ± 0.1 ps (48 %), τ2 = 9 ± 1 ps (33 %), τ3 = 60 ± 10 ps (10 %) and τ4 = 3 ± 1 ns (9%)) (Fig. S14d), the decay lifetime of CPDs in 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite material is largely extended (τ1 = 4 ± 1 ps (19 %),τ2 = 70 ± 10 ps (27 %) and τ3 > 5 ns (54 %)) (Fig. 3c), which is because the holes of CPDs in composite can recombine with the electrons of Bi4O5Br2 through the intemal electric field (Fig. 3d), thereby reducing the recombination probability of photo-generated carriers in CPDs and prolonging the lifetime of photo-generated electrons on the surface of CPDs. The TAS effectively prove the charge separation in the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite heterojunction with direct Z-scheme pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical, electronics and CO2 adsorption of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was used to further investigate the interfacial charge transfer resistance of pure Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs modified Bi4O5Br2 manterials (Fig. 4 a). The charge transfer resistance of the electrode surface is evaluated by the diameter of semicircle in Nyquist plot, which indicate a smaller diameter means a lower resistance [44]. As the content of CPDs increases, the Nyquist plot diameter of composite gradually decreases, indicating that the resistance gradually decreases. This is mainly contributed that the conjugated π structure of CPDs acted as effective transporters to accelerate the interfacial charge transfer [23,30]. Figs. 4b–c and S15 display the transient photocurrent spectra of these materials. Under Xe lamp irradiation (Fig. 4b), all CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 materials exhibited higher photocurrent intensity than that of pure Bi4O5Br2. Compared with 5 and 10 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2, the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 showed the highest photocurrent response nearly with 0.27 μA, which is probably because the fewer CPDs cannot separate carriers efficiently, and excessive CPDs could affect the light absorption of Bi4O5Br2 that reduce the productivity of photogenerated electrons [45]. Under λ ≥ 400 nm light irradiation (Fig. S15), the photocurrent intensity of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 is about 6.2 times than that of pure Bi4O5Br2. It could infer that the direct Z-scheme CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction could accelerate the separation of photogenerated carriers. The samples were irradiated under λ ≥ 580 nm for further exploration of carrier separation (Fig. 4c), the photocurrents of Bi4O5Br2, 8 wt% CNTs/Bi4O5Br2 (Fig. S16) and 8 wt% CPDs/SiO2 (Fig. S17) are almost undetectable. This is because the Bi4O5Br2 material cannot be excited by light with λ > 500 nm (Fig. 1c). Although the light absorption range of the CPDs can reach the near-infrared region, the high carrier recombination efficiency results in weak photocurrent signal of CPDs. Only 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 exhibits a significant photocurrent response. This is because the up-conversion performance of CPDs can excite the electronic transition of Bi4O5Br2, and the effective separation of photogenerated carriers for composite via the direct Z-scheme heterojunction system can be promoted significantly. This is consistent with the subsequent photocatalytic CO2 conversion activity. Moreover, the CO2 adsorption capacity is generally regarded as another important prerequisite during the CO2 photoreduction process. The 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 material exhibit higher CO2 adsorption isotherms than that of original Bi4O5Br2 (Fig. 4d), indicating CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite had more prominent advantage in CO2 adsorption/concentration and suggesting the greater CO2 conversion potential for CPDs/Bi4O5Br2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of photocatalytic CO2 conversion activity
ce:section.ce:para: Using solar light as a driving force, CO2 is raw material for synthesizing carbon-based fuels, which is great significance in mitigating energy crisis and environmental pollution. In the system, the photocatalytic CO2 conversion experiments were carried out in pure CO2 (about 88 kPa) without any sacrificial reagent under different light irradiation (300 W Xe lamp and visible light (λ ≥ 400 nm and λ ≥ 580 nm)). The main product of photocatalytic CO2 conversion is determined to be CO by gas chromatography. And no products are detected in the reaction solution. As observed in Fig. 5 a–b, the pristine Bi4O5Br2 showed the lowest CO yield and 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 material exhibited the highest conversion efficiency of CO2 to CO. Under Xe lamp irradiation for three hours, the CO production rates of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 (132.42 μmol h−1 g−1) is 5.43 fold enhancement compared to pure Bi4O5Br2 (24.37 μmol h−1 g−1), revealing the significant enhancement in photocatalytic CO2 conversion activity of the direct Z-scheme CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite. The activity of the direct Z-scheme system is much higher than that of Bi4O5Br2 monomer and composite system in the literatures (Table S1). Compared with the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2, the CO production rates of 5 wt% and 10 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 are 58.16 and 78.88 μmol h−1 g−1, respectively. This superior CO generation rate of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 complex is also higher than many of the literatures (Table S2), such as (1) Co single atom loaded Bi3O4Br atomic layer (107.1 μmol g−1 h−1) and Bi3O4Br atomic layer (27.0 μmol g−1 h−1) (light source: 300 W Xenon lamp) [15]. (2) Oxygen-defect BiOBr atomic layers (87.4 μmol g−1 h−1) (light source: 300 W Xenon lamp) [14]. (3) CPDs modified PbBiO2Br heterojunction (8.28 μmol g−1 h−1) (light source: 300 W Xenon lamp) [23]. (4) N-rich carbon quantum dots/dualphase TiO2 heterostructured nanocomposites (23.66 μmol g−1 h−1) (light source: 300 W Xenon lamp) [46]. The apparent quantum efficiency of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 at 380, 400 and 420 nm is calculated to be 0.81 %, 0.37 % and 0.133 % within 3 h, respectively (Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 also exhibits excellent stability (Fig. S18). With continuous Xe lamp illumination for 32 h, the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite still maintained excellent photocatalytic CO2 conversion performance. The invariable XRD patterns (Fig. S19a) and micromorphology (Fig. S19b-c) of the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 materials before and after photocatalytic experiments further confirm the superior durability of catalyst. The 13C isotope labelling experiment explore the carbon source of evolved CO, the 13CO peak at m/z = 29 in the mass spectra, demonstrating the CO product originate from the photocatalytic splitting of CO2 molecules (Fig. 5d and S20). Moreover, the peak at m/z = 32 in the mass spectra can be assigned to 16O2, which indicates photogenerated holes of the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite material can oxidize H2O to form O2 in the photocatalytic process. According to the band structure of Bi4O5Br2 and CPDs, the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite may follow carrier migration and separation through the direct Z-scheme mechanism (Fig. S21). The related mechanism speculation combined with the electron spin resonance analysis is shown in the Fig. S22.

Further research on visible light catalytic CO2 conversion performance indicates the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 also exhibited excellent photocatalytic CO2 conversion properties. Under visible light with λ ≥ 400 nm irradiation (Fig. 5e and f left), the photocatalytic CO2 conversion properties of 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 (19.45 μmol h−1 g−1) is still far exceed the CO generation rate of monomer Bi4O5Br2 (4.08 μmol h−1 g−1). The CNTs/Bi4O5Br2 (Fig. S16) and CPDs/SiO2 (Fig. S17) composites were constructed to compare the performance of different heterojunctions systems. After replacing CPDs with CNTs, the photocatalytic activity of 8 wt% CNTs/Bi4O5Br2 (7.85 μmol h−1 g−1) showed a certain improvement compared with monomer Bi4O5Br2, but much lower than CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 system, indicating that the introduction of CPDs construct direct Z-scheme CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction is more conducive to enhancing the photocatalytic activity. In addition, when CPDs is combined with insulator SiO2, the photocatalytic properties of the 8 wt% CPDs/SiO2 composites are weak (1.17 μmol h−1 g−1), which may be attributed to the CPDs with narrow band gap and no additional separation migration route for photogenerated carriers that is prone to recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Further comparison has been executed under visible light with λ ≥ 580 nm irradiation (Fig. 5f right), the photocatalytic CO2 conversion of 8 wt% CNTs/Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2 and 8 wt% CPDs/SiO2 have almost no detectable CO product, while 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 still maintains high CO generation rate of 2.48 μmol h−1 g−1. The up-conversion performance of CPDs can excite the electronic transition of Bi4O5Br2, and the band matching of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 with direct Z-scheme mechanism can promote the effective separation of photogenerated electrons. Thus CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 effectively broadens the utilization range of visible light and significantly improves photocatalytic CO2 conversion performance.

The CO2 photoreduction process has been investigated using in situ FT-IR measurements. As shown in Fig. 6 a–b, the absorption peaks at 1319, 1473 and 1510 cm−1 are belong to the monodentate carbonate groups (m-CO3 2-), at 1418 and 1437 cm−1 are attributable to the bicarbonate (HCO3-) [47,48]. And the peaks at 1340, 1356, 1556 and 1565 cm−1 are assigned to the characteristic bands of COOH groups [14], which has been generally regarded as the important intermediate during CO2 reduction to CO. Both Bi4O5Br2 and 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 exhibited a series of IR characteristic peaks, suggesting they underwent the same CO2 reduction reaction pathways. Compared with the pure Bi4O5Br2, the 8 wt% CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 showed the obviously higher IR absorption spectrum intensity, which indicated more production of COOH* groups on the surface of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2. It may signify that the introduction of CPDs is beneficial to stabilize more COOH* intermediate on the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2, which can effectively reduce the activation barrier of CO2 transformation [23]. The IR absorption peak from 2320 to 2370 cm−1 are associated with the asymmetric stretching of CO2 that decreased gradually with illumination time (Fig. 6c), which indicates that CO2 is gradually reduced on the surface of these catalysts [49]. The peaks at 1800−2200 cm−1 are attributed to the characteristic adsorption peak of CO (Fig. 6d) [50,51]. With the process of photoreduction reaction, the characteristic adsorption peak of CO for Bi4O5Br2 keep increasing. Interestingly, the characteristic adsorption peak of CO for CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 reaches maximum after illumination, then begin to reduce until up to balance. This may be attributed to the reduced reaction barrier from CO2 to COOH* for CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites (reaction 2), and then further electron reduction COOH* resulted in the instantaneous enrichment of CO* on the surface of CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 catalyst (reaction 3). The introduction of CPDs could decreases the adsorption energy of CO on the composite material, which makes the CO desorption rate on the catalyst surface larger than the adsorption rate at this time. Therefore, at the initial stage of photocatalytic CO2 conversion reaction, the IR absorption spectrum intensity of CO on the surface of compound material decreases gradually. With the reaction proceeding, CO desorption/desorption on the hybrid catalyst surface gradually balanced, then the intensity of IR absorption spectra also gradually stabilized. Thus, CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 catalyst with enhanced stabilization of the COOH* intermediate and desorption capacity of CO, which can reduce the activation energy of CO2 and increase the productivity of the final CO. The most likely CO2 reduction path for the catalytic system could be as follows:
(1) CO2 (g) → CO2*
(2) CO2* + e− + H+ → COOH*
(3) COOH* + e− + H+ → CO* + H2O
(4) CO* → CO

Based on the above analysis of energy band structure, electron transfer path and intermediate products, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic CO2 reduction was proposed. When the illumination wavelength is less than 500 nm (Fig. 6e), electrons on VB of both CPDs and Bi4O5Br2 can be excited to transition to CB of them. Due to the internal electric field in contact interface between CPDs and Bi4O5Br2, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of Bi4O5Br2 will combine with the holes on the VB of CPDs via direct Z-scheme photocatalytic electron transfer system. The electrons on the CB of CPDs can reduce CO2 to form CO, and the holes on the VB of Bi4O5Br2 can oxidize water to form O2. In addition, under illumination of light greater than 500 nm, the electrons on the VB of Bi4O5Br2 cannot be excited, while the electrons on the VB of CPDs can transition to the CB. Meanwhile, CPDs with up-conversion performance can emit light of less than 500 nm (Fig. 6f), which can excite Bi4O5Br2 to have the electronic transition. Then, the composite catalyst transfer electron via the direct Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism, and the redox reaction is carried out. To this end, the CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite material effectively broadens the utilization range of visible light, improves light utilization efficiency, and achieves high visible light catalytic CO2 conversion rate.

In summary, CPDs was successfully introduced on the surface of Bi4O5Br2 to form CPDs/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst with direct Z-scheme photocatalytic CO2 conversion system. On the one hand, it is beneficial to the effective separation of photogenerated carriers, and on the other hand, it effectively broadens the utilization range of visible light. In addition, the introduction of CPDs also promotes the adsorption of CO2, stabilization of intermediate product COOH*, and desorption of product CO, which effectively reduces the reaction energy barrier of CO2 conversion to CO. The possible photocatalytic mechanisms have been proposed. This work opens a boulevard for the preparation of highly efficient visible or even infrared-driven CO2 conversion catalysts.
